statement.p: Let \mathbb{P}_3 be the vector space of polynomials of degree at most 3. Determine which of the following are bases for this vector space.

ol.li:
Yes. Suppose
c_1(x^3+1)+c_2(x^2+x)+c_3(2x^3+x^2)+c_4(2x^3-x^2-3x+1)=0.
Then collect equal powers of x:
(c_1+2c_3+2c_4)x^3+(c_2+c_3-c_4)x^2+(c_2-3c_4)x+(c_1+c_4)=0.
Does the system
c_1+2c_3+2c_4 \amp =0, c_2+c_3-c_4\amp =0, c_2-3c_4\amp =0, c_1+c_4\amp =0
have a non-trivial solution? The only solution is c_1=0, c_2=0, c_3=0, c_4=0, and therefore, the polynomials are linearly independent. Since there are 4 linearly independent polynomials in a 4-dimensional space, they form a basis.

statement.p: Determine whether the following is a basis for \mathbb{P}_2, the vector space of polynomials of degree at most 2: \left\{x^2+x+1, 2x^2+2x+1, x+1\right\}.

Find a basis for the following subspace of \mathbb{P}_2:
W=\mathrm{span}\{1+x+x^2,\;1+2x,\;1+5x-3x^2\}.
Extend the following linearly independent set of polynomials to a basis of \mathbb{P}_3: \left\{x^3+x^2-x-1, 3x^3+2x^2+2x-1\right\}.
Let V be a 5-dimensional vector space. If you have 5 linearly independent vectors in V, can you conclude that the vectors span V?

solution.p: Yes, because the set of 5 linearly independent vectors can be extended to a basis B of V. But since V is 5-dimensional, B has only 5 elements, which must be the original 5 vectors.

statement.p: Let V be a 5-dimensional vector space. If you have 6 vectors in V, is it possible that they are linearly independent? Explain.

solution.p: No. Since V has a spanning set of size 5, the 6 vectors cannot be linearly independent by the Exchange Lemma.

statement.p: Find a basis for the vector space of symmetric 3\times 3-matrices, i.e., matrices satisfying A=A^T. What is the dimension of this space?
Let W be the subspace of P_3 (over the field \mathbb{R}) consisting of all polynomials p(x) that satisfy p(3)=0. Find a basis for W. What is the dimension of W?

Find a basis for
U=\left\{ A\in M_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}) \ \middle|\ A\begin{bmatrix}1\amp0\\1\amp-1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1\amp1\\0\amp-1\end{bmatrix}A \right\}.
What is the dimension of U?
